answer:
I have a clear picture now. Let me give a complete answer.

## What happens when you call `response.content` on a streaming response

Looking at `httpx/_models.py` line 635-639, `response.content` is a simple property:

```python
@property
def content(self) -> bytes:
    if not hasattr(self, "_content"):
        raise ResponseNotRead()
    return self._content
```

**It does NOT trigger the stream to be read.** If `_content` hasn't been populated yet, it raises `ResponseNotRead` (defined in `httpx/_exceptions.py:340`):

> "Attempted to access streaming response content, without having called `read()`."

This is a fundamental difference from non-streaming requests — when you use `httpx.get(...)` or `client.get(...)`, the library reads the body for you and sets `_content`, so `.content` just works. When you use `client.stream(...)` / `httpx.stream(...)`, it's left to you.

## The lifecycle you need to respect

The `Response` object has two key flags (set up in `_models.py` around line 544):

- `is_stream_consumed` — set to `True` once the underlying stream has been iterated (see `iter_raw` at line 939-946, which raises `StreamConsumed` if you try again)
- `is_closed` — set to `True` when the stream is closed (`iter_raw` raises `StreamClosed` if you try to read after close, line 941-942)

So the valid lifecycle inside a `with httpx.stream(...)` block is:

**Option A — Stream chunk-by-chunk (don't use `.content`/`.text`):**
```python
with httpx.stream("GET", url) as r:
    for chunk in r.iter_bytes():   # or iter_text / iter_lines / iter_raw
        ...
# r.content here would raise ResponseNotRead
```

**Option B — Buffer the whole body, then use `.content`/`.text`:**
```python
with httpx.stream("GET", url) as r:
    r.read()           # drains the stream into r._content
    print(r.content)   # now works
    print(r.text)      # now works too
```

`read()` (line 876-882) is what bridges streaming mode and the `.content` attribute — it joins `iter_bytes()` into `self._content`. The quickstart docs (lines 375-382) show the idiomatic pattern of conditionally calling `r.read()` based on e.g. `Content-Length`.

## The rules in one list

1. **You can only iterate the body once.** Calling `iter_bytes`/`iter_raw`/`read` a second time raises `StreamConsumed`.
2. **You must read before the context manager exits.** Once the `with` block closes the response, `is_closed` becomes `True` and any further attempt to iterate raises `StreamClosed`. (However, if you already called `.read()`, `.content` remains valid after close because it's cached on `_content`.)
3. **`.content` never drains the stream itself** — it only returns a cached `_content`. No `_content` ⇒ `ResponseNotRead`.
4. **`.text` depends on `.content`** (line 642-650) — same rule applies; it raises `ResponseNotRead` on a streaming response that hasn't been `read()`.
5. **Async analogue:** use `await r.aread()` and `async for ... in r.aiter_bytes()` inside `async with client.stream(...)`. The sync-vs-async mismatch is enforced too (line 943-944: "Attempted to call a sync iterator on an async stream.").

### TL;DR

`response.content` on an un-read streaming response raises `httpx.ResponseNotRead`. To use it, call `response.read()` (or `await response.aread()`) first — that drains the stream once, caches it in `_content`, and makes `.content`/`.text` available for the rest of the response's life. Do it inside the `with`/`async with` block, and don't try to iterate the stream again afterwards.

336:## Streaming Responses
338:For large downloads you may want to use streaming responses that do not load the entire response body into memory at once.
375:If you're using streaming responses in any of these ways then the `response.content` and `response.text` attributes will not be available, and will raise errors if accessed. However you can also use the response streaming functionality to conditionally load the response body:

336	## Streaming Responses
   337	
   338	For large downloads you may want to use streaming responses that do not load the entire response body into memory at once.
   339	
   340	You can stream the binary content of the response...
   341	
   342	```pycon
   343	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   344	...     for data in r.iter_bytes():
   345	...         print(data)
   346	```
   347	
   348	Or the text of the response...
   349	
   350	```pycon
   351	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   352	...     for text in r.iter_text():
   353	...         print(text)
   354	```
   355	
   356	Or stream the text, on a line-by-line basis...
   357	
   358	```pycon
   359	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   360	...     for line in r.iter_lines():
   361	...         print(line)
   362	```
   363	
   364	HTTPX will use universal line endings, normalising all cases to `\n`.
   365	
   366	In some cases you might want to access the raw bytes on the response without applying any HTTP content decoding. In this case any content encoding that the web server has applied such as `gzip`, `deflate`, `brotli`, or `zstd` will
   367	not be automatically decoded.
   368	
   369	```pycon
   370	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   371	...     for chunk in r.iter_raw():
   372	...         print(chunk)
   373	```
   374	
   375	If you're using streaming responses in any of these ways then the `response.content` and `response.text` attributes will not be available, and will raise errors if accessed. However you can also use the response streaming functionality to conditionally load the response body:
   376	
   377	```pycon
   378	>>> with httpx.stream("GET", "https://www.example.com") as r:
   379	...     if int(r.headers['Content-Length']) < TOO_LONG:
   380	...         r.read()
   381	...         print(r.text)
   382	```
   383	
   384	## Cookies
   385	
   386	Any cookies that are set on the response can be easily accessed:
   387	
   388	```pycon
   389	>>> r = httpx.get('https://httpbin.org/cookies/set?chocolate=chip')
   390	>>> r.cookies['chocolate']
   391	'chip'
   392	```
   393	
   394	To include cookies in an outgoing request, use the `cookies` parameter:
   395
